item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same. If I remember this for any reason in a week's time, it will be because "aetheric flux" reminds me of quantum flux, which sustains us all. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Although I do enjoy this SCP, it was a solid "meh" for me. It's not deserving of a down vote, but I wasn't really interested as much as others. This is a no-vote for me.

Edit: You know what; upon a second reading, I've changed my mind.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The picture of the interior is clearly that of the Sagrada Família. I'd like to enjoy the rest of this, but that particular image kind of puts me off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are SCPs that have done better at evoking wonder and/or terror, but for what this is, it's positively delicious: The Foundation does Ghostbusters with a generous helping of surprisingly competent Gothic horror that evokes Poe and Lovecraft in all the right ways. Fantastic job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted somewhere around "aetheric monopole," because that is the kind of thaumobabble that successfully evokes something really, really strange even if I don't have the reference frame to parse it into a literal meaning.

Decided to upvote several additional times throughout reading, including one for the last interview log and one for the incident report. Vaguely disappointed that this proved impossible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How about the fact that quarks come in flavors of up, down, strange, charm, top, and bottom?

I work in physics. Stuff like this is super common.

In other news, Reality is Unrealistic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not recursive, but I think my favorite computer tech acronym (really backronym) is TWAIN: Technology Without An Interesting Name. Mostly used to get scanners to properly interface with computers, and originally named for the line "and never the twain shall meet" because of how annoyingly difficult it was to get it working properly.

So yeah, world's full of fun, silly and absolutely ridiculous names for industry-specific stuff. The Foundationverse is more realistic for having some silly names, not less… But I guess that some people want things to have sexier names or something, so the argument will continue forever.

I have yet to see people come up with better names, though, even with all the complaining. Guess that's pretty much the acid test for the community.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh, didn't know that about how quarks were named. Thanks for that!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yup, the kilocaspers stood out to me as a nice touch, simply because it is exactly the kind of thing that does happen in real life.

(how about recursive acronyms in computer science? GNU's Not Unix; PINE Is Not Elm, Wine Is Not an Emulator, CAVE Automatic Virtual Environment,YAML Ain't Markup Language, etc)

though my favorite SCP unit of measurement is still "humes", on the "kafka scale".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And so is the fact that the unit for processing speed is "flops" (floating-point operations per second, which is totally not a backronym). The point is that it's stupid in a plausible way. ^_^

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Happens in physics, too. For instance, one of the most fundamental particles, the quark, comes from a line in James Joyce's Finnegan's Wake. It takes three quarks (two up and one down for a proton, two down and one up for a neutron) to make a hadron, and this caused one of the model's inventors, Murray Gell-Mann, to recall a line from his favorite novel - "Three quarks for Muster Clark!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's still stupid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well there's no particular reason to use any given element from another work, there is no canon. I was just curious if the author had a specific reason to create a new MTF with apparently the same mission profile (and even a same-sourced nickname — though I think "Who you gonna call?" is the better one).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What you're missing is that I was being dickish in a manner that was entirely uncalled-for. The implication, detached though it may be, was meant entirely as an insult to the author and I should've just kept my mouth shut.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Having dealt with scientists momentarily, I agree that the academia of Natural Science tend to have rather "lame" tendencies in humour.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To me Caspers isn't LOLFoundation, but just another bit of realism- in-jokes are really, really common in science: "Lucy" is named for Lucy In The Sky With Diamonds, Ranasmurfin is a blue pigment named after The Smurfs; Bastardane is an unwanted exception to standard hydrocarbon linking rules that causes problems in hydrocarbon processing, Draculin is an anticoagulant found in vampire bat saliva; diethyl azodicarboxylate is an volatile, carcinogenic, shock sensitive explosive and cellular irritant abbreviated DEAD…

My personal favorite is still the Sonic Hedgehog gene (and associated protein). A scientist needed a type of hedgehog for his newly discovered gene in the "Hedgehog" signaling group family and his daughter had a new favorite comic book. Turns out to be one of the most important genes in vertebrate embryo development, and now you have hundreds of serious scientific papers like "Sonic hedgehog synergizes with the extracellular matrix protein vitronectin to induce spinal motor neuron differentiation.". Most people on both sides of the joke find this highly amusing. X3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really? Now I want to roll my eyes more (which will not affect my vote). Guess it's the same case as the protein named "Pikachu".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The casper is, in fact, a unit used elsewhere on site (mainly the GOC canon) to describe magic radiation. As such, it'd be kind of a stupid reason to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I balked at all the "magic" tech used in the containment procedures, to say nothing of all the cross-linking for the purposes of explaining what the hell was going on, but decided to give it a chance because that is kind of accepted these days.

What clinched the downvote was, believe it or not, "kilocaspers". Just too silly, threw all that suspension of disbelief out the window. I just can't take this seriously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Theta-77 is disinformation? I did not get that at all from reading this. What am I missing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not a fan. It feels like scraps of half a dozen gothic horror stories, reconfigured in a way that isn't particularly new or interesting. Nothing about this surprised me or made me curious to read more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was interesting when I read it as a draft, but the pictures really improved on the overall quality. You should have added them ages ago!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: <hr />

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What is the difference between MTF Theta-77 and Mu-13?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, a few things here.

First, I'm just going to say that the canon-exclusive form of this one kinda throws me.

But two points on the general writing: One, you use the term EVE once or twice before you explain what it means. Two, you've still got an SCP-XXXX in there.

But I think what makes me really not care much for this article is the fact that it's just giant frickin' wall of text after giant frickin' wall of text. I tried… I really did… but I just couldn't make it past the first sentence of the addendum.

I'd say that I really loved the history of the building, as it gave me basically everything I need to know about the story behind the SCP, while maybe deviating a bit from general Foundation tone, but whatever. It was good, and I liked it. Everything before that point seemed extremely (…for some reason the only word coming into my head right now is "extraneous"), and seemed like a lot of reading without much payout. Furthermore, the Keter properties of this SCP are just sorta unceremoniously piled onto the thing without much real need for it. The article would be a lot shorter and a much better read in my opinion if the danger of the thing were bumped down to Euclid levels.

To sum it up, I'd say that the real gold here is the story of a man just trying to hang on to his loved ones. That needs to be the focus of the article, and it should be your hook if you can somehow get it up there. I know it hurts to chop up something you worked really hard on, but I'd say that you're going to have to if you want this to really do well.

I'm gonna have to call a no-vote on this one. I hope to see it again-hopefully a bit more streamlined-in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely love it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like I said when I was reviewing this as a draft, this article is a lot of fun. The Gothic themes remind me a lot of Edgar Allan Poe, and the paratech is very interesting to read about. All in all a solid article.

+1 minmin, glad this is finally up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is terrific. There's a lot of nice fun jargon to read and the story ends up being pretty nifty too. Good form, minmin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Friggen awesome. Detailed paratech, great imagery, heaps of style, concise narrative that ties everything together… one hell of an SCP. This is one of those that shapes canons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's well written, but it didn't particularly interest me.
Specifically, it almost seems cliché.

Not a fan.

item.title: it's a haunted victorian mansion located in dark peak, how can this be anything but cheesy
item.content:encoded:
Image credits
– hide block
  • First photo, edited by tawny from synIRC. Licensed under CC 2.0. Photo by user China Crisis on Wikipedia.
  • Second photo from public domain.
  • Third photo is also public domain.
  • Fourth image's skull, licensed under CC 3.0. Photo by user Nevit Dilmen on Wikipedia.
  • Fourth image's sigil, photoshopped in by myself, licensed under CC 3.0. Originally by user Schwerdf on Wikipedia, who traced it from a public domain image.
  • Last photo is cropped and edited by me. Licensed under CC 2.0. Original photographer is user SBA73 on Flickr.

Thanks to all who read the draft in chat - in no particular order: ARD, Conwell, Jekeled, Metaphysician, Kalinin, TwistedGears, TyGently, Greenwolf, and ztshp - your feedback all helped tremendously. Here's hoping this will be a good one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Consequently, SCP-2696-1 exhibits much higher levels of aspect rradiation

Minor typo. No complaints otherwise
